s1_b1: A perfect machine: every bit of work you put in comes out the other side.
s1_b2: Build it in the real world and something is always missing.
s1_b3: You crank a real winch. Some of your effort lifts the load. Some just warms the gears.
s1_b4: It didn't vanish. Friction turned part of your work into heat and sound. Useless for the job.
s1_b5: So a machine has two stories. The work you hoped for. And the work you actually got.
s1_b6: The ratio of what you got to what you put in — that's efficiency.
s1_b7: A perfect machine would be one hundred percent. Nothing real ever is.
s1_b8: A good gearbox might keep ninety. A clumsy chain of parts, far less.
s1_b9: And in a compound machine the losses stack. Each stage skims a little off the top.
s1_b10: This is why ideal mechanical advantage and the real one disagree. The gap is exactly what friction ate.
s1_b11: Energy is still conserved — none was destroyed. It just left the job and became heat.
s1_b12: Computing efficiency as useful output over total input, and the work lost to friction in a real machine — that's yours.
